APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper addresses a clearly relevant NLP task—text categorization—using discourse (RST) structure and a novel attention-enabled recursive neural architecture, which fits the scope and audience of top NLP/ML venues. The methodology uses expected modern components (BiLSTM EDUs, relation-specific composition, and comprehensive baselines including HAN and prior discourse models) and evaluates on multiple standard datasets of varying sizes, meeting community evaluation standards. From a reproducibility/methodology standpoint there are some concerns (no code mentioned, sensitivity to RST treebank size is acknowledged), which prevent a perfect fit but do not outweigh the paper’s topical relevance and empirical rigor. | B(5): This paper fits well within a top-tier NLP/ML venue: it addresses a core NLP problem (text categorization) while introducing a discourse-informed neural architecture grounded in RST, which is of clear interest to both modeling and discourse communities. The methodology (EDU BiLSTM, relation-specific composition, novel unnormalized attention) and evaluation across multiple standard datasets and baselines meet venue expectations, and the authors explicitly analyze limitations (sensitivity to discourse training size) and situate the work within related literature. From a discourse-and-applications perspective, the paper targets the right audience and is positioned to advance discourse-aware methods in mainstream NLP research.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses a clearly relevant NLP task—text categorization—using discourse (RST) structure and a novel attention-enabled recursive neural architecture, which fits the scope and audience of top NLP/ML venues. The methodology uses expected modern components (BiLSTM EDUs, relation-specific composition, and comprehensive baselines including HAN and prior discourse models) and evaluates on multiple standard datasets of varying sizes, meeting community evaluation standards. From a reproducibility/methodology standpoint there are some concerns (no code mentioned, sensitivity to RST treebank size is acknowledged), which prevent a perfect fit but do not outweigh the paper’s topical relevance and empirical rigor.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper fits well within a top-tier NLP/ML venue: it addresses a core NLP problem (text categorization) while introducing a discourse-informed neural architecture grounded in RST, which is of clear interest to both modeling and discourse communities. The methodology (EDU BiLSTM, relation-specific composition, novel unnormalized attention) and evaluation across multiple standard datasets and baselines meet venue expectations, and the authors explicitly analyze limitations (sensitivity to discourse training size) and situate the work within related literature. From a discourse-and-applications perspective, the paper targets the right audience and is positioned to advance discourse-aware methods in mainstream NLP research.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares to a broad set of relevant baselines (Hierarchical Attention Networks, several prior discourse-weighting models, Yogatama & Smith, Card et al., Yessenalina et al.) across multiple datasets using consistent metrics (accuracy, 10-fold CV). Comparisons appear fair with reported hyperparameters and dataset/metric consistency, and the paper explicitly acknowledges cases where discourse parsing quality hurts performance. One minor gap is no mention of very recent neural/Transformer baselines or released code for exact reproducibility, so comparisons are strong but not fully comprehensive by current standards. | B(5): The paper compares against a wide and relevant set of baselines including hierarchical attention (Yang et al., 2016), multiple prior discourse-weighting approaches (Bhatia et al., Hogenboom et al.), structured methods (Yogatama & Smith), and task-specific baselines (Card et al., Yessenalina et al.), covering both recent and discourse-focused work. Comparisons appear fair (same datasets, accuracy metrics, cross-validation reported, hyperparameters given), the authors explicitly acknowledge when prior methods can match or outperform theirs under reduced discourse training data, and they analyze distinctions between their relation-aware recursive composition and attention variants (labeled vs. unlabeled). The paper also cites and discusses the most relevant competing approaches, so overall the experimental comparison is comprehensive and well-justified.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares to a broad set of relevant baselines (Hierarchical Attention Networks, several prior discourse-weighting models, Yogatama & Smith, Card et al., Yessenalina et al.) across multiple datasets using consistent metrics (accuracy, 10-fold CV). Comparisons appear fair with reported hyperparameters and dataset/metric consistency, and the paper explicitly acknowledges cases where discourse parsing quality hurts performance. One minor gap is no mention of very recent neural/Transformer baselines or released code for exact reproducibility, so comparisons are strong but not fully comprehensive by current standards.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against a wide and relevant set of baselines including hierarchical attention (Yang et al., 2016), multiple prior discourse-weighting approaches (Bhatia et al., Hogenboom et al.), structured methods (Yogatama & Smith), and task-specific baselines (Card et al., Yessenalina et al.), covering both recent and discourse-focused work. Comparisons appear fair (same datasets, accuracy metrics, cross-validation reported, hyperparameters given), the authors explicitly acknowledge when prior methods can match or outperform theirs under reduced discourse training data, and they analyze distinctions between their relation-aware recursive composition and attention variants (labeled vs. unlabeled). The paper also cites and discusses the most relevant competing approaches, so overall the experimental comparison is comprehensive and well-justified.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper runs across a broad set of datasets (large-scale 650k and several smaller, domain-specific corpora) and compares to both simple and strong baselines (HAN, prior discourse and lexical models), which supports the core claim. The authors include meaningful ablations/sensitivity checks (relation-labeled vs relation-agnostic variants and a study reducing RST treebank size) and explicitly discuss a key limitation (dependence on discourse training data quality/size). However, reproducibility is incomplete (code not mentioned, and runtime/compute and statistical significance details are not reported), so while the experimental evaluation is solid, minor gaps prevent it being fully comprehensive. | B(4): The paper runs extensive experiments across multiple domains (news, debates, movie reviews, bills, large Zhang et al. corpus) and compares to a wide set of baselines, supporting the central claim that RST-based discourse helps classification. It includes sensible ablations (labeled vs unlabeled relations), reports hyperparameters, and analyzes sensitivity to discourse-treebank size — showing awareness of parser-dependence. Related work coverage is thorough and failure modes/limitations are explicitly discussed. One remaining gap for full methodological completeness is explicit code release or more robustness checks to parser error types and domain transfer beyond the reported treebank-size sensitivity.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper runs across a broad set of datasets (large-scale 650k and several smaller, domain-specific corpora) and compares to both simple and strong baselines (HAN, prior discourse and lexical models), which supports the core claim. The authors include meaningful ablations/sensitivity checks (relation-labeled vs relation-agnostic variants and a study reducing RST treebank size) and explicitly discuss a key limitation (dependence on discourse training data quality/size). However, reproducibility is incomplete (code not mentioned, and runtime/compute and statistical significance details are not reported), so while the experimental evaluation is solid, minor gaps prevent it being fully comprehensive.

It includes sensible ablations (labeled vs unlabeled relations), reports hyperparameters, and analyzes sensitivity to discourse-treebank size — showing awareness of parser-dependence. Related work coverage is thorough and failure modes/limitations are explicitly discussed. One remaining gap for full methodological completeness is explicit code release or more robustness checks to parser error types and domain transfer beyond the reported treebank-size sensitivity.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is generally well organized and reads logically from motivation through method to experiments and conclusion; the main contributions (use of RST discourse with a recursive network and novel unnormalized attention) are stated early and repeatedly. Notation appears consistent and the method description (EDU BiLSTM, relation-specific composition matrices, sigmoid attention) is precise enough to follow; hyperparameters are reported which aids reproducibility. Figures/tables and examples are reported as effective and the related work coverage is thorough, though the absence of mentioned code release and a few places (e.g., sensitivity to discourse treebank size) suggest some experimental details/implications could be presented more prominently for clarity. Overall the writing quality is good with only minor presentation shortcomings. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early and followed by a clear method->experiments->conclusion flow; the summary reports a coherent description of the recursive RST-based model, the EDU BiLSTM and the novel unnormalized attention. Notation and key equations (e.g., the sigmoid attention formulation) are presented and hyperparameters are reported, which aids reproducibility. Related work coverage is thorough and writing quality is described as good, but the summary does not confirm the effectiveness of figures/tables or whether any minor ambiguities remain in complex sections (e.g., robustness to parser error), so I judge it very clear overall with only minor presentation issues.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is generally well organized and reads logically from motivation through method to experiments and conclusion; the main contributions (use of RST discourse with a recursive network and novel unnormalized attention) are stated early and repeatedly. Notation appears consistent and the method description (EDU BiLSTM, relation-specific composition matrices, sigmoid attention) is precise enough to follow; hyperparameters are reported which aids reproducibility. Overall the writing quality is good with only minor presentation shortcomings.

Notation and key equations (e.g., the sigmoid attention formulation) are presented and hyperparameters are reported, which aids reproducibility. Related work coverage is thorough and writing quality is described as good, but the summary does not confirm the effectiveness of figures/tables or whether any minor ambiguities remain in complex sections (e.g., robustness to parser error), so I judge it very clear overall with only minor presentation issues.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper addresses a meaningful problem—incorporating RST discourse structure into text categorization—and demonstrates practical gains across diverse datasets (large Zhang et al. corpus, MFC, Congressional debates, movie reviews, bills) with comparisons to strong baselines (HAN, prior discourse and lexical models). Its novel unnormalized attention and recursive RST-based architecture provide a concrete technique researchers can build on, but the impact is constrained by reliance on an external RST parser and the sensitivity to discourse-treebank size (performance drops if the RST data is reduced), which limits immediate broad applicability. Overall this is a solid, useful contribution that is likely to influence follow-up work but not fundamentally change the field.  | B(4): This work addresses an important applied problem—how to incorporate RST discourse structure into document classification—and provides a concrete neural architecture and novel unnormalized attention mechanism that demonstrably leverages discourse trees across diverse datasets (from large-scale Zhang et al. data to MFC, Congressional debates, movie reviews, and legislative bills). The finding that discourse-aware recursive composition improves classification, together with the analysis of labeled vs. relation-agnostic variants and the noted sensitivity to discourse treebank size, should change how researchers think about using discourse parsers (and weighing parser quality) in downstream tasks and motivates follow-up work on parser-robustness and interpretable attention. Given the practical applicability to multiple domains and the introduction of a new attention formulation tied to linguistic structure, the paper is likely to be built upon by others, though its dependence on discourse-annotated resources limits fully transformative reach.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses a meaningful problem—incorporating RST discourse structure into text categorization—and demonstrates practical gains across diverse datasets (large Zhang et al. corpus, MFC, Congressional debates, movie reviews, bills) with comparisons to strong baselines (HAN, prior discourse and lexical models). Its novel unnormalized attention and recursive RST-based architecture provide a concrete technique researchers can build on, but the impact is constrained by reliance on an external RST parser and the sensitivity to discourse-treebank size (performance drops if the RST data is reduced), which limits immediate broad applicability. Overall this is a solid, useful contribution that is likely to influence follow-up work but not fundamentally change the field.

reviewer_b.justification: This work addresses an important applied problem—how to incorporate RST discourse structure into document classification—and provides a concrete neural architecture and novel unnormalized attention mechanism that demonstrably leverages discourse trees across diverse datasets (from large-scale Zhang et al. data to MFC, Congressional debates, movie reviews, and legislative bills). The finding that discourse-aware recursive composition improves classification, together with the analysis of labeled vs. relation-agnostic variants and the noted sensitivity to discourse treebank size, should change how researchers think about using discourse parsers (and weighing parser quality) in downstream tasks and motivates follow-up work on parser-robustness and interpretable attention. Given the practical applicability to multiple domains and the introduction of a new attention formulation tied to linguistic structure, the paper is likely to be built upon by others, though its dependence on discourse-annotated resources limits fully transformative reach.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper combines known elements (RST discourse trees, EDU BiLSTMs, recursive composition) with a novel twist—an unnormalized sigmoid-based attention on discourse dependency children—but the overall idea of leveraging discourse for classification has prior art (Bhatia et al., Hogenboom et al., Yessenalina et al.). The relation-specific composition matrices and the particular unnormalized attention are non-trivial engineering contributions, yet they read as an incremental advance rather than a fundamentally new paradigm. From a reproducibility/novelty standpoint this would inform experts but is not highly surprising given the cited prior work. | B(3): The paper combines known components (EDU BiLSTMs, RST parser output, and recursive tree composition) but introduces a distinct twist: an unnormalized (sigmoid) attention mechanism over discourse-tree children to avoid forced competition among siblings. This is a non-trivial, practically motivated extension beyond prior discourse-weighting and hierarchical attention work, and the labeled vs. unlabeled analysis and dependence-on-treebank-size findings are useful differentiators. However, the overall approach remains largely an incremental integration of existing techniques rather than a fundamentally new paradigm, so its novelty is moderate rather than high.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper combines known elements (RST discourse trees, EDU BiLSTMs, recursive composition) with a novel twist—an unnormalized sigmoid-based attention on discourse dependency children—but the overall idea of leveraging discourse for classification has prior art (Bhatia et al., Hogenboom et al., Yessenalina et al.). The relation-specific composition matrices and the particular unnormalized attention are non-trivial engineering contributions, yet they read as an incremental advance rather than a fundamentally new paradigm. From a reproducibility/novelty standpoint this would inform experts but is not highly surprising given the cited prior work.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper combines known components (EDU BiLSTMs, RST parser output, and recursive tree composition) but introduces a distinct twist: an unnormalized (sigmoid) attention mechanism over discourse-tree children to avoid forced competition among siblings. This is a non-trivial, practically motivated extension beyond prior discourse-weighting and hierarchical attention work, and the labeled vs. unlabeled analysis and dependence-on-treebank-size findings are useful differentiators. However, the overall approach remains largely an incremental integration of existing techniques rather than a fundamentally new paradigm, so its novelty is moderate rather than high.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and largely supported by empirical comparisons against reasonable baselines (HAN, several discourse-weighting methods, and logistic-regression variants) across multiple datasets, and it analyzes labeled vs. unlabeled discourse variants and data-size sensitivity. The model formulation (EDU BiLSTM, relation-specific composition, and an unnormalized sigmoid attention) is described and appears internally consistent, and hyperparameters are reported. However, the summary does not mention released code, random seeds, or statistical significance testing, and reproducibility risks are noted (performance sensitivity to RST treebank size), which are minor but important gaps for fully validating the claims. | B(4): The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and largely supported by experiments across multiple datasets and reasonable baselines (HAN, prior discourse-weighting and structured models), and the novel unnormalized attention is well integrated into the recursive RST-based network. Mathematical formulation of the composition and attention appears coherent; experiments include both labeled/unlabeled variants and report results on several corpora including a large 650k dataset, which strengthens empirical validity. However, the summary omits mention of statistical significance tests and the model’s robustness to parser errors/domain transfer is only partially acknowledged (performance degrades with reduced RST training data), representing a modest gap in evidence. Overall the technical approach is sound but would benefit from additional robustness analysis and explicit statistical evaluation.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and largely supported by empirical comparisons against reasonable baselines (HAN, several discourse-weighting methods, and logistic-regression variants) across multiple datasets, and it analyzes labeled vs. unlabeled discourse variants and data-size sensitivity. The model formulation (EDU BiLSTM, relation-specific composition, and an unnormalized sigmoid attention) is described and appears internally consistent, and hyperparameters are reported. However, the summary does not mention released code, random seeds, or statistical significance testing, and reproducibility risks are noted (performance sensitivity to RST treebank size), which are minor but important gaps for fully validating the claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and largely supported by experiments across multiple datasets and reasonable baselines (HAN, prior discourse-weighting and structured models), and the novel unnormalized attention is well integrated into the recursive RST-based network. Mathematical formulation of the composition and attention appears coherent; experiments include both labeled/unlabeled variants and report results on several corpora including a large 650k dataset, which strengthens empirical validity. However, the summary omits mention of statistical significance tests and the model’s robustness to parser errors/domain transfer is only partially acknowledged (performance degrades with reduced RST training data), representing a modest gap in evidence. Overall the technical approach is sound but would benefit from additional robustness analysis and explicit statistical evaluation.

main_claims: Discourse structure as defined by Rhetorical Structure Theory (RST) and provided by an existing discourse parser benefits text categorization.

A recursive neural network built on a discourse dependency tree with an EDU-level BiLSTM representation and a novel (unnormalized) attention mechanism can learn to weight salient EDUs for classification.

The proposed attention mechanism automates weighting previously done by hand and differs from standard normalized attention by not forcing competition among siblings; experiments include labeled and unlabeled (relation-agnostic) variants and analyze strengths and weaknesses.
_summary.methodology: Build a recursive neural network on discourse dependency trees produced by an RST parser; compute EDU vectors with a bidirectional LSTM and compose parent and children representations via relation-specific matrices and an attention weight (sigmoid of e_i^T W_alpha v_j) to form a document representation used for classification.
datasets: preprocessed dataset from Zhang et al. (2015) (650,000 train, 50,000 test)
Media Frames Corpus (MFC; Card et al., 2015) (~4,200 news articles about immigration)
Congressional floor debates corpus (Thomas et al., 2006; split by Yessenalina et al., 2010)
Movie review corpus (Pang and Lee, 2004) (1,000 positive and 1,000 negative reviews)
Legislative bills dataset (Yano et al., 2012) (51,762 bills)
baselines: Hogenboom et al. (2015) discourse-weighting / lexicon-based model
Card et al. (2016) logistic-regression based model with unigrams/bigrams/personas
Yessenalina et al. (2010) latent-variable informativeness model
limitations: Performance depends on the quality/size of the discourse training data: if the RST discourse treebank were reduced to 25% of its size, the proposed method would underperform a discourse-ignorant model (Yang et al., 2016).
_summary.topic_area: NLP - text categorization using discourse structure (RST)

reviewer_a.name: Practical Methodologist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with a PhD in NLP who has led reproducible benchmark evaluations and industrial deployments of text-classification systems.
traits: insists on strong, well-justified baselines (including simple and state-of-the-art) and clear experimental protocols
looks for comprehensive ablation studies that isolate contributions of model components and training choices
checks statistical significance, dataset splits, hyperparameter sensitivity, and runtime/compute costs for practicality
values reproducibility: requests detailed implementation details, hyperparameters, and publicly available code or seeds
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on empirical rigor and reproducibility, assessing whether the experiments reliably support the paper's claims and whether others can reproduce the results.

reviewer_b.name: Discourse & Applications Specialist
reviewer_b.background: Senior NLP researcher with extensive publications on discourse analysis, RST, and applied text understanding, and experience connecting linguistic theories to downstream tasks.
traits: evaluates novelty and conceptual fit with prior discourse-aware and structured neural models
examines whether the paper meaningfully advances understanding of how discourse structure contributes to task performance
attends to realistic applicability: robustness to parser errors, domain transfer, and interpretability of the proposed attention
values clear analysis connecting model behavior to linguistic phenomena and implications for future work
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Assesses the paper's novelty, theoretical grounding in discourse literature, and broader impact on discourse-informed NLP methods and applications.
